sentences.base_language: Sofía has had a toothache for three days. She finally makes an appointment.
sentences.target_language: Sofía lleva tres días con dolor de muela. Por fin pide una cita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llevar + time + con/gerund** expresses how long something has been going on:
lleva tres días con dolor = She's had the pain for three days.
llevo dos horas esperando = I've been waiting for two hours.

sentences.base_language: She arrives at the dentist's office and checks in.
sentences.target_language: Llega al consultorio del dentista y se registra.
sentences.grammarNote:
**al consultorio** — **al** is a contraction of **a + el** (always required when **a** precedes **el**).
**del dentista** — **del** is a contraction of **de + el**.
**se registra** — reflexive **registrarse** (to check in/register). The reflexive **se** shows the action is done to/for oneself.
**el consultorio** (office/clinic) — used for medical offices; **la oficina** is for general offices.

sentences.base_language: The dentist comes in. 'Good morning, Sofía. Which tooth is bothering you?'
sentences.target_language: El dentista entra. —Buenos días, Sofía. ¿Qué muela le duele?
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿Qué muela le duele?** — **doler** works like gustar: the tooth (**muela**) is the subject that 'hurts to her' (**le**).
**la muela** (molar) vs. **el diente** (tooth/incisor) — dentists often use **muela** for back teeth specifically.
The dentist uses **le** (usted indirect object) — formal register with the patient.

sentences.base_language: 'This one on the lower right side. It hurts a lot when I eat.'
sentences.target_language: —Esta de abajo a la derecha. Me duele mucho cuando como.
sentences.grammarNote: **Me duele cuando como** — note the present tense in the time clause (cuando + present), not the infinitive. In Spanish, habitual 'when I...' uses the indicative present: cuando como, cuando hablo, cuando camino.

sentences.base_language: 'Let's take a look. Open wide, please.'
sentences.target_language: —Vamos a ver. Abra bien la boca, por favor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Vamos a ver** (let's see / let's take a look) — **ir a + infinitive** for near future, but also used as a fixed expression meaning 'let's see.'
**Abra** is a formal (usted) affirmative command of **abrir** — irregular: the -ir verb uses -a ending: **abra** (not *abre*).
**bien** here intensifies the command — 'open [it] wide/well.' **por favor** placed after the command softens the instruction.

sentences.base_language: The dentist examines the tooth carefully.
sentences.target_language: El dentista examina la muela con cuidado.
sentences.grammarNote:
**con cuidado** (carefully) — literally 'with care.' Spanish often uses **con + noun** where English uses an adverb: **con cuidado** (carefully), **con rapidez** (quickly), **con facilidad** (easily).
**examinar** (to examine) — regular -ar verb, a direct borrowing from Latin. Related English cognate: 'examine,' 'examination.'
**la muela** (the tooth/molar) — definite article used for the specific tooth in question.

sentences.base_language: 'I can see the problem. You have a small cavity here.'
sentences.target_language: —Ya veo el problema. Tiene una pequeña caries aquí.
sentences.grammarNote:
**La caries** (cavity) is feminine and ends in -s in both singular and plural: la caries / las caries.
Same pattern for nouns ending in unstressed -es: la crisis / las crisis, el análisis / los análisis.

**¿Es grave?** — **ser** is used for inherent characteristics: seriousness/severity is a property of the condition.
Contrast: **¿Está peor?** (Is it worse? — temporary state) uses **estar**.
**grave** (serious/severe) is invariable for gender — same form masculine and feminine.
Common medical adjectives: **leve** (mild) · **moderado** (moderate) · **grave** (serious) · **crítico** (critical).

sentences.base_language: 'Not at all, it's small. I'll need to do a filling. It'll take about twenty minutes.'
sentences.target_language: —Para nada, es pequeña. Tengo que hacerle un empaste. Tardará unos veinte minutos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para nada** (not at all) — stronger negation than just **no**.
**Tengo que + infinitive** (I have to / I need to) — common obligation expression.
**hacerle** — indirect object **le** attached to the infinitive: 'to do for you' (the filling is done for the patient).
**Tardará** is the future tense of **tardar** (to take time/to be late): **tardará unos veinte minutos** (it will take about twenty minutes). **unos** = approximately.
**¿Va a doler?** uses ir a + infinitive for the near future — same as English 'going to.' **Doler** is an o→ue stem-changer: duele, duelen. The infinitive after ir a has no stem change.

sentences.base_language: 'I'll give you a little anesthesia first. You'll barely feel a thing.'
sentences.target_language: —Primero le voy a poner un poco de anestesia. Apenas va a sentir algo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**le voy a poner** — indirect object **le** (to you, formal) + **ir a + infinitive** (going to put).
**un poco de anestesia** — **un poco de + noun** (a little bit of): un poco de anestesia, un poco de agua, un poco de tiempo.
**Apenas va a sentir algo** — **apenas** (barely/scarcely) + future construction: 'you'll barely feel anything.' Note **algo** (something) in an affirmative sense — in negative sentences it becomes **nada**: no va a sentir nada.

sentences.base_language: Sofía relaxes a bit. After twenty minutes, it's all done.
sentences.target_language: Sofía se relaja un poco. Después de veinte minutos, todo termina.
sentences.grammarNote:
**se relaja** — reflexive **relajarse** (to relax oneself): se relaja, me relajo, te relajas.
Contrast: **relajar** (to relax something/someone else): el masaje me relaja (the massage relaxes me).
**después de + time** (after + time period) — **Después de veinte minutos** (after twenty minutes).
**todo termina** (everything/it all ends) — present tense used for a vivid/narrative past: storytelling in the present.

sentences.base_language: 'That wasn't so bad! I should have come sooner.'
sentences.target_language: —¡No fue tan malo! Debería haber venido antes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**No fue tan malo** — **fue** is the preterite of **ser** (was): irregular preterite **fui, fuiste, fue, fuimos, fuisteis, fueron** (same forms as **ir** in the preterite — context distinguishes).
**Debería haber venido** — conditional perfect construction: **debería** (should/conditional of deber) + **haber venido** (perfect infinitive = to have come). Used for past regrets: 'I should have come sooner.'
**antes** (sooner/before) — adverb of time; here means 'earlier/sooner.'
